channel.title: The Agentic State: AI in Government Service Delivery on CasaClawd Research
channel.description: Recent content in The Agentic State: AI in Government Service Delivery on CasaClawd Research

item.title: Executive Summary
item.description:
Bottom Line

Stanley Milgram’s agentic state—where people surrender moral responsibility to authority figures—has a direct parallel in modern government AI systems. When public servants defer to algorithmic recommendations, they enter an algorithmic agentic state: responsibility shifts to “the system,” critical thinking atrophies, and harmful decisions get rubber-stamped.

New insight from Ilves et al. (2025): There’s a second meaning of “Agentic State” emerging—the transformation of government itself through autonomous AI systems. This creates a paradox: the Agentic State (as governance model) may inadvertently create agentic states (as psychological phenomenon) in public servants.

item.title: Core Concepts
item.description:
The Original Agentic State (Milgram)

Stanley Milgram’s obedience experiments revealed that ordinary people will harm others when:

  1. An authority figure gives orders
  2. The person enters an agentic state (acts as instrument of authority)
  3. Responsibility is deferred upward
  4. Moral strain is experienced but overridden

Key insight: It’s not cruelty that enables harm—it’s surrendering moral agency.

The Algorithmic Agentic State

When AI enters government decision-making, a parallel phenomenon emerges:

Element Milgram’s Lab Government AI
Authority Experimenter in lab coat Algorithm/system output
Command “Continue the shocks” “System flagged this case”
Agentic shift “I’m following orders” “The algorithm decided”
Responsibility Deferred to experimenter Deferred to system/vendor
Moral strain Visible distress Known errors, continued use
Defense “I was just following orders” “I was following the system”

Automation Bias

Definition: The tendency to over-rely on automated recommendations and ignore contradictory information, even when correct.

item.title: Two Meanings of Agentic State
item.description:
Meaning 1: The Psychological Agentic State (Milgram)

Origin: Stanley Milgram’s obedience experiments (1963)

Definition: A mental state where an individual acts as an agent of an authority figure, deferring moral responsibility upward. The person sees themselves as an instrument carrying out another’s wishes, not as a morally responsible individual.

In government AI context: Public servants deferring to algorithmic recommendations, rubber-stamping AI decisions, shifting responsibility to “the system.”

Meaning 2: The Governance Agentic State (Ilves et al., 2025)

Origin: Vision paper by Global Government Technology Centre Berlin & The World Bank (October 2025)

item.title: Case Studies
item.description:
Case Study 1: Robodebt (Australia, 2016-2020)

What Happened

The Australian Government implemented an automated debt recovery system for Centrelink welfare payments:

  • Algorithm compared fortnightly welfare data with annual tax data
  • Income averaging created false debts for people with irregular income
  • 470,000+ incorrect debt notices issued
  • A$1.8 billion unlawfully claimed
  • Multiple suicides linked to the scheme

The Algorithmic Agentic State in Action

The Authority: The “system”—automated debt calculation algorithm

The Agentic Shift:

item.title: Governance & Safeguards
item.description:
Australian Government AI Governance Framework

Current Policy Landscape (2025)

1. Australia’s AI Ethics Principles (2019)

  • Human, social and environmental wellbeing
  • Human-centered values
  • Fairness
  • Privacy protection and security
  • Reliability and safety
  • Transparency and explainability
  • Contestability
  • Accountability

2. Policy for Responsible AI in Government (v2.0, December 2025)

  • Mandatory for Australian Government agencies
  • Risk-based approach to AI use
  • Transparency requirements
  • Human oversight mandates

3. Services Australia AI Strategy 2025-27

  • Governance and assurance processes before deployment
  • Transparency statement on AI use
  • Alignment with whole-of-government frameworks

Safeguards Against Algorithmic Agentic States

Technical Safeguards

Safeguard Purpose
Explainable AI (XAI) Officials can understand AI reasoning
Confidence Scoring Flag uncertain predictions
Audit Logs Track all AI decisions and overrides
Bias Testing Identify discriminatory patterns

Human Safeguards

Safeguard Purpose
Mandatory Override Training Staff know they can question AI
Human-in-the-Loop Required human review for high-stakes
Whistleblower Protections Safe to raise concerns

Red Flags: When Governance is Failing

Technical Red Flags

  • AI decisions can’t be explained to affected citizens
  • No confidence scores or uncertainty measures
  • No audit trail of decisions

Cultural Red Flags

  • “The algorithm decided” is common phrase
  • Staff stop questioning AI outputs
  • “Just following the system” defenses

Recommendations for Australian Government

Immediate Actions

  1. Expand Robodebt Royal Commission recommendations

item.title: Sources & References
item.description:
Primary Source: The Agentic State Vision Paper

Ilves, L., Kilian, M., Parazzoli, S.M., Peixoto, T.C., & Velsberg, O. (October 2025)

  • The Agentic State: Rethinking Government for the Era of Agentic AI
  • Global Government Technology Centre Berlin & The World Bank
  • Download PDF

Key contributions:

  • Dual meaning of “Agentic State” (governance transformation vs. psychological phenomenon)
  • 12-layer framework for implementation
  • 6-level autonomy spectrum (0-5)
  • The paradox: Agentic State governance may create agentic state psychology

Australian Case Studies

  1. UQ Business School - Robodebt Analysis
